script: I spent three years building the perfect productivity system

props.before: A perfect productivity system — second brain, time-blocks, color-coded calendars, 17-step morning routines
props.after: Seven small habits simple enough to do on bad days
props.label: The system had become the work

script: the system had become the work
My shutdown ritual takes four minutes

tips.text: Write one line on where you'll pick up tomorrow
Note one thing you actually finished today

script: David Allen calls these open loops

props.definition: Unfinished tasks your brain keeps tracking until it registers closure (Zeigarnik effect).
props.analogy: Like browser tabs left open — each one quietly drains memory in the background.

script: The value is in the emptying, not the filing
The done list corrects your perception of yourself

props.before: To-do list — a permanent state of deficit that never hits zero
props.after: Done list — five or six meaningful things you actually moved forward
props.label: Correct your perception of yourself

script: one 90-minute block of deep work per day

props.definition: One protected deep-work session per day — the first 15-20 minutes are just warming up, so short blocks end right as your brain hits its stride.
props.analogy: Treat it like a meeting you can't cancel.

script: The night before, write exactly three priorities for tomorrow

tips.text: Not five — five means you don't have to choose
Not ten — that's a task list, not a priority list
Three — write it the night before, while you still have context

script: These habits don't ask for perfect. They ask for honest

props.quote: They don't ask for perfect. They ask for honest. And honest, practiced every day, compounds in ways no color-coded calendar ever matched.

script: Start with one — the one that stings a little
